sections.plain_summary: Surgical adhesions are bands of scar tissue that form between internal organs and tissues after abdominal or pelvic surgery, infection, or inflammation. They can bind together structures that are normally separate — such as loops of bowel, the ovaries, or the bladder — causing pain, digestive problems, and in serious cases, bowel obstruction or fertility issues. Adhesions are extremely common after abdominal surgery but are rarely discussed as a standalone diagnosis, which leaves many people searching for years to explain their ongoing symptoms.
sections.detailed_summary:
When the body heals from surgery, trauma, infection, or inflammation inside the abdominal or pelvic cavity, it lays down fibrous scar tissue as part of the normal repair process. In most people, some of this tissue forms adhesions — web-like bands that bridge surfaces that should remain free to move independently. These can involve the bowel, the peritoneum (the lining of the abdominal cavity), the ovaries, fallopian tubes, bladder, or any other pelvic and abdominal structure.

Adhesions are estimated to occur in 90% of people who have had major abdominal surgery, yet they are notoriously under-recognised as a cause of ongoing symptoms. This is partly because they are invisible to most imaging: standard CT scans and ultrasounds generally cannot detect adhesions directly — they become visible only when they cause a complication like bowel obstruction. This creates a diagnostic gap where people experience real, often debilitating symptoms with no identifiable cause on standard workups.

The mechanism of symptoms varies. Adhesions can tether and pull on bowel, causing cramping, altered transit, bloating, and nausea. They can obstruct bowel partially or completely. In the pelvis, they can restrict organ movement and cause chronic pelvic pain, especially during movement, sex, or menstruation. They can compress nerves or trap them in scar tissue, creating neuropathic pain. Some adhesions remain silent for years before becoming symptomatic, often triggered by a second surgery, infection, or inflammation.

Treatment is complicated. The primary intervention — surgical lysis (cutting) of adhesions — carries a well-established risk of forming new adhesions in the process of removal. This means that surgery to relieve adhesion symptoms can cause future adhesion symptoms. For many people, conservative management, physiotherapy, and careful symptom management offer a more sustainable path than repeated surgery. A small number of centres offer specialised adhesion assessment and minimally invasive lysis with anti-adhesion barriers, which may reduce recurrence risk.

prevalence.summary: Adhesions form in the vast majority of people who have abdominal or pelvic surgery, but only a subset develop chronic symptoms.
prevalence.estimate: Adhesions form in roughly 90% of people after open abdominal surgery and 55–100% after gynaecological surgery; approximately 5–10% of those develop chronic pain or bowel symptoms attributable to adhesions.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence data is complicated by the fact that many adhesions are asymptomatic and only discovered incidentally during later surgery. Chronic adhesion-related pain is likely substantially under-diagnosed because of the difficulty in confirming adhesions without surgery. Women are disproportionately affected due to higher rates of pelvic surgery, endometriosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease.

subgroups.name: Post-surgical adhesions (abdominal)
subgroups.description: Adhesions that develop following any laparotomy or laparoscopic abdominal procedure, most commonly appendectomy, bowel resection, hernia repair, or cholecystectomy.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms often begin weeks to years after surgery
May cause partial or complete small bowel obstruction
Colicky abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits are common
History of surgery is usually clear but causal link is often not made by treating clinicians

subgroups.name: Pelvic adhesions (gynaecological)
subgroups.description: Adhesions within the pelvis, commonly involving the ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, and bowel, often arising from endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, or gynaecological surgery.
distinguishing_features: Deep pelvic pain, often worse with menstruation or intercourse
Associated with infertility
May coexist with active endometriosis, making attribution difficult
Often missed because pelvic ultrasound does not detect adhesions reliably

subgroups.name: Adhesion-related bowel obstruction
subgroups.description: The most medically serious subgroup, where adhesions cause partial or complete mechanical obstruction of the small bowel.
distinguishing_features: Recurrent episodes of severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension
May resolve spontaneously or require urgent surgical intervention
CT scan may show dilated bowel loops consistent with obstruction
Often requires hospitalisation; a surgical emergency in complete obstruction

subgroups.name: Adhesion-related chronic pain syndrome
subgroups.description: A pattern where adhesions contribute to persistent pain without causing frank obstruction, often involving neuropathic or referred pain components.
distinguishing_features: Constant or intermittent pain disproportionate to findings on standard imaging
Pain often worsens with movement, position change, or digestion
Central sensitisation may develop over time, amplifying pain signals
Typically requires a multidisciplinary approach including pain specialists and physiotherapy

subgroups.name: Post-Caesarean and post-hysterectomy adhesions
subgroups.description: Adhesions specific to uterine or bladder involvement following Caesarean section or hysterectomy.
distinguishing_features: Bladder involvement can cause urinary symptoms including urgency and incomplete emptying
Lower abdominal tightness or pulling sensation, especially on standing or walking
Scar tissue at the uterovesical fold may cause bladder-related pelvic pain
Can complicate future surgery significantly

symptoms.notes: Often described as cramping, pulling, or tightening. May worsen after eating, with movement, or during bowel activity. Can be constant or episodic.
May be related to impaired bowel transit or partial obstruction. Often worse throughout the day and relieved (partially) by passing gas or bowel movements.

symptoms.symptom: Altered bowel habits — constipation, diarrhoea, or alternating
symptoms.notes: Adhesions can slow or disrupt intestinal transit. Constipation is more common when adhesions tether or narrow bowel loops.
Often associated with meals or with pain episodes. Can be a sign of partial obstruction.

symptoms.symptom: Episodes of severe colicky pain with vomiting
symptoms.notes: Episodic severe pain with vomiting may indicate intermittent partial bowel obstruction — this warrants urgent assessment.
Constant or intermittent. Often worse with movement, sexual intercourse, menstruation, or bladder filling. May be bilateral or localised.

symptoms.symptom: Deep dyspareunia (pain during or after intercourse)
symptoms.notes: Particularly associated with pelvic adhesions involving the ovaries, uterosacral ligaments, or rectovaginal space.
Adhesions can restrict uterine movement and tether pelvic organs, amplifying menstrual pain. Often confused with or comorbid with endometriosis.
Pelvic adhesions involving the fallopian tubes or ovaries may impair egg transport or ovarian function.

symptoms.symptom: Pulling sensation at or near a surgical scar
symptoms.notes: Many people describe a tethering or dragging feeling directly under or around old scars, especially with bending, stretching, or exercise.
Pelvic adhesions can transmit tension to the sacral ligaments and pelvic floor, causing referred low back pain.

symptoms.symptom: Urinary urgency or incomplete bladder emptying
symptoms.notes: Can occur when adhesions tether the bladder, particularly after Caesarean section or hysterectomy.
Chronic pain conditions commonly produce fatigue through sleep disruption, nervous system dysregulation, and the metabolic cost of persistent pain.
Common in people with undiagnosed chronic pain. Often secondary to years of not being believed or given a clear diagnosis.

key_patterns: History of abdominal or pelvic surgery, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, or peritonitis preceding the onset of symptoms
Chronic pain that standard imaging (CT, ultrasound) fails to explain
Symptoms that worsen with bowel activity, movement, or position changes in a post-surgical patient
Episodic severe pain with nausea and vomiting consistent with intermittent partial small bowel obstruction
Infertility or deep pelvic pain in someone with a history of pelvic surgery or pelvic inflammatory disease
CT scan showing dilated small bowel loops with a transition point but no mass or other structural cause

at_home.name: Symptom and surgery timeline diary
at_home.description: Keeping a detailed written record of the timing of symptoms relative to surgical history, position changes, meals, bowel movements, and menstrual cycle can help establish patterns and aid clinical assessment. Note whether pain is worse with standing, walking, sitting, or after eating.
at_home.safety_notes: This is observational only. If severe colicky pain occurs with vomiting and inability to pass gas or stool, seek emergency assessment immediately — this may indicate bowel obstruction.

at_home.name: Scar tissue mobility self-assessment
at_home.description: Gently assessing whether the skin and tissue around old abdominal scars feels restricted, tethered, or painful to light touch or gentle movement can help identify surface-level adhesion involvement. This is not diagnostic but may inform conversations with a physiotherapist.
at_home.safety_notes: Do not apply significant pressure to post-surgical areas without professional guidance. This assessment is superficial and does not reflect deep internal adhesions.

clinical.name: CT scan of abdomen and pelvis
clinical.description: The most common imaging used to investigate suspected bowel obstruction. Can show dilated bowel loops with a transition point suggesting adhesion-related obstruction. However, CT cannot directly visualise adhesions themselves — it only shows their consequences. A normal CT does not rule out adhesions.
clinical.availability: Widely available; typically ordered by a gastroenterologist, general surgeon, or emergency physician.

clinical.name: Cine MRI (dynamic MRI)
clinical.description: A specialised MRI technique that assesses the movement (sliding motion) of abdominal organs relative to each other. Reduced or absent sliding between organ surfaces is an indirect marker of adhesion formation. This is the closest non-invasive test to detecting adhesions directly.
clinical.availability: Available at specialist centres in the Netherlands and a small number of other centres internationally. Not routinely available in most hospitals. May require a referral to a centre with expertise in adhesion assessment.

clinical.name: Diagnostic laparoscopy
clinical.description: Direct visualisation of the abdominal and pelvic cavity under general anaesthesia. This remains the only definitive way to confirm the presence, location, and extent of adhesions. However, it carries the risk of forming new adhesions and is therefore not undertaken lightly.
clinical.availability: Performed by gynaecologists or general/colorectal surgeons. Used when non-invasive investigations are inconclusive and symptoms are significantly impairing quality of life.

clinical.name: Small bowel follow-through / fluoroscopy
clinical.description: A contrast X-ray study that tracks the movement of barium or water-soluble contrast through the small intestine. Can identify areas of narrowing or delayed transit consistent with adhesion-related obstruction or tethering.
clinical.availability: Available at most hospitals with a radiology department. Ordered by gastroenterologists or surgeons.

clinical.name: Pelvic ultrasound with sliding sign assessment
clinical.description: A technique used by experienced sonographers to assess whether the uterus and ovaries slide freely against surrounding structures during gentle probe pressure. Absence of the normal sliding sign ('kissing ovaries' or fixed pelvic organs) suggests pelvic adhesions. Operator-dependent and not universally available.
clinical.availability: Best performed at specialist endometriosis or pelvic pain centres where sonographers are trained in this technique.

clinical.name: Gynaecological assessment and hysteroscopy
clinical.description: Relevant for intrauterine adhesions (Asherman's syndrome) causing menstrual abnormalities or infertility. Hysteroscopy allows direct visualisation of adhesions within the uterine cavity.
clinical.availability: Performed by gynaecologists; available at fertility and gynaecology clinics.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis is one of the most common causes of pelvic adhesions. Endometrial implants cause chronic inflammation and fibrosis that leads to adhesion formation. The two conditions often coexist and can be difficult to distinguish clinically without laparoscopy.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Post-surgical adhesions causing altered bowel habits, bloating, and abdominal pain are frequently misdiagnosed as IBS. The key distinguishing feature is the clear temporal relationship with prior surgery, but this link is often not made by clinicians.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Adhesions that tether or narrow sections of bowel can impair peristalsis and promote bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine. SIBO may develop as a consequence of adhesion-related dysmotility and can contribute significantly to bloating and altered bowel habits.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic floor dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Pelvic adhesions can alter the mechanics of pelvic organ movement and load distribution, contributing to pelvic floor muscle dysfunction, hypertonia, and pain. The two conditions frequently coexist and each can drive the other.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Persistent pain from adhesions can sensitise the central nervous system over time, leading to amplified pain responses that persist even when the structural source of pain is treated. This is an important reason why adhesion lysis does not always fully resolve pain.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Adhesions involving the stomach or proximal small bowel can impair gastric emptying mechanically. Additionally, post-surgical autonomic nerve disruption can cause delayed gastric emptying alongside adhesion formation.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic undiagnosed pain, repeated failed investigations, and being dismissed by clinicians commonly generates significant anxiety in people with adhesions. Anxiety also lowers the pain threshold and can amplify symptom severity.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Long-term chronic pain from adhesions, combined with diagnostic delays and functional limitations, is associated with high rates of depression. Depression in turn amplifies pain perception and worsens quality of life.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Central sensitisation arising from unresolved adhesion pain can overlap with or contribute to fibromyalgia-like widespread pain syndromes. Both conditions involve amplified pain processing.

related_conditions.name: IBD (Crohn's disease / ulcerative colitis)
related_conditions.explanation: Inflammatory bowel disease, particularly Crohn's disease, causes transmural inflammation and repeated surgical interventions that substantially increase adhesion risk. Adhesion-related symptoms can be difficult to distinguish from IBD flares.

related_conditions.name: Hormonal / menstrual cycle effects
related_conditions.explanation: Pelvic adhesions frequently cause cyclically varying pain that worsens with menstruation, as hormonal changes increase pelvic congestion and tissue tension in tethered structures.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cells are involved in the fibrotic process that drives adhesion formation. Additionally, MCAS can amplify pain and inflammatory responses in scar tissue. Some researchers believe mast cell activity may influence adhesion severity.

related_conditions.name: Asherman's syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Intrauterine adhesions (Asherman's syndrome) represent adhesion formation specifically within the uterine cavity, typically following uterine surgery or infection. It shares the same fundamental pathophysiology as external pelvic adhesions.

related_conditions.name: Small bowel obstruction
related_conditions.explanation: Adhesions are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction in adults in developed countries, accounting for approximately 60–70% of cases. Obstruction is the most serious complication of adhesive disease.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
related_conditions.explanation: Infection and inflammation from PID is a major non-surgical cause of pelvic adhesions, particularly those involving the fallopian tubes and ovaries.

related_conditions.name: Peritonitis
related_conditions.explanation: Any cause of peritoneal inflammation — including ruptured appendix, perforated bowel, or abdominal infection — triggers widespread fibrous adhesion formation as a healing response.

strategies.name: Visceral physiotherapy / abdominal scar tissue mobilisation
strategies.description: Specialised physiotherapists trained in visceral manipulation work to address the mechanical effects of adhesions on organ mobility and surrounding soft tissue. Techniques include gentle manual work on scars, fascial release, and assessment of organ mobility. This does not remove adhesions but may reduce the tension and tethering they cause on surrounding structures. Evidence is limited but many individuals report significant symptom improvement.
strategies.notes: Seek practitioners specifically trained in visceral manipulation or pelvic physiotherapy with post-surgical experience. Mainstream physiotherapy without this specialisation is unlikely to address adhesion-specific issues.

strategies.name: Pain management — multimodal approach
strategies.description: Chronic adhesion pain often requires a combination of approaches: low-dose naltrexone (LDN) has some evidence in chronic pelvic pain; neuropathic agents such as amitriptyline or gabapentin can help where nerve entrapment or central sensitisation is involved; non-opioid analgesics for acute episodes. Opioids carry significant risks and are generally not suitable for long-term adhesion pain management.
strategies.notes: Pain management should be coordinated by a pain specialist or clinician experienced in chronic pelvic pain. Opioid escalation should be avoided where possible given poor long-term outcomes and the risk of narcotic bowel syndrome.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP or easily digestible diet during symptom flares
strategies.description: When adhesions impair bowel transit, reducing fermentable carbohydrates and increasing the proportion of low-residue foods can reduce gas production, bloating, and distension. Smaller, more frequent meals may also reduce the load on a partially restricted bowel.
strategies.notes: This is symptomatic management rather than treatment of adhesions themselves. A FODMAP-trained dietitian can help tailor the approach and avoid unnecessary long-term dietary restriction.

strategies.name: Treating secondary SIBO if present
strategies.description: If bacterial overgrowth has developed secondary to adhesion-related dysmotility, treatment with appropriate antibiotics (most commonly rifaximin) under medical supervision may significantly improve bloating and bowel symptoms, even if adhesions cannot be removed.
strategies.notes: SIBO is likely to recur if the underlying dysmotility from adhesions is not addressed. Prokinetics may be needed as maintenance therapy after SIBO treatment.

strategies.name: Pelvic floor physiotherapy
strategies.description: When pelvic adhesions have contributed to pelvic floor hypertonicity or dysfunction, specialist pelvic floor physiotherapy can reduce muscular guarding, pain with intercourse, and pelvic heaviness. Internal and external techniques are used.
strategies.notes: Best undertaken by a pelvic floor physiotherapist with experience in post-surgical pelvic pain. Should be distinguished from standard physiotherapy.

strategies.name: Surgical adhesiolysis (lysis of adhesions)
strategies.description: Surgical cutting or removal of adhesions, typically by laparoscopy, can provide relief — particularly for clearly obstructive adhesions or those causing severe tethering. Anti-adhesion barriers applied during surgery may reduce the risk of adhesion reformation. However, surgery itself stimulates new adhesion formation, meaning symptom relief may be temporary.
strategies.notes: The decision to operate should be made carefully by a surgeon experienced in adhesion management, weighing the risk of new adhesion formation against the severity of current symptoms. Repeated adhesiolysis may become less effective over time. Best undertaken only at centres with experience in adhesion surgery and access to anti-adhesion barriers.

strategies.name: Psychological support and pain neuroscience education
strategies.description: Understanding the role of central sensitisation and how chronic pain reshapes the nervous system can help reduce fear-avoidance behaviours and improve functional capacity. CBT, acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT), and pain neuroscience education programmes have demonstrated benefit in chronic pelvic and abdominal pain conditions.
strategies.notes: This is not the same as being told the pain is psychological. It is an evidence-based approach to managing the amplification of real structural pain signals. It works best alongside, not instead of, structural assessment and management.

strategies.name: Bowel obstruction emergency protocol awareness
strategies.description: People with adhesion history should understand the warning signs of bowel obstruction (severe colicky pain, vomiting, abdominal distension, inability to pass gas or stool) and know to seek emergency assessment immediately. Early intervention dramatically improves outcomes.
strategies.notes: This is not optional for anyone with a history of adhesion-related bowel obstruction or significant adhesive disease.

things_to_avoid: Repeated unnecessary abdominal surgery for pain alone without specialist adhesion assessment — each operation carries significant risk of forming new adhesions
Long-term opioid escalation for adhesion pain — carries risk of narcotic bowel syndrome, which causes increasing rather than decreasing pain
Ignoring symptoms of acute bowel obstruction (severe pain, vomiting, distension, no passage of gas or stool) — this requires emergency evaluation
Assuming that normal CT or ultrasound rules out clinically significant adhesions — standard imaging cannot visualise adhesions directly
High-residue, high-fibre diets during active bowel symptoms if partial obstruction is suspected — increasing fibre can worsen obstruction
Accepting an IBS diagnosis without review of surgical history if symptoms began or worsened after abdominal or pelvic surgery
Delaying physiotherapy or manual therapy treatment — scar tissue may become less amenable to mobilisation over time if left untreated

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The only definitive way to confirm adhesions is surgery — and surgery to remove adhesions creates a high risk of forming new ones. There is no clean fix.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People in pain naturally want a procedure that resolves the problem. Surgeons are trained to operate. The idea that the best treatment may be to avoid more surgery is counterintuitive and hard to accept when the pain is severe.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who accept this reality and work with conservative management — visceral physiotherapy, pain neuroscience, careful dietary management, and SIBO treatment if relevant — often achieve significantly better long-term quality of life than those who pursue repeated surgery. The goal shifts from cure to managing a structural condition as effectively as possible with the least risk of worsening it.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many mainstream doctors will not name adhesions as the cause of ongoing pain, even when the surgical history makes it highly plausible, because they cannot prove it without operating.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Being told there is nothing wrong on the scans when the pain is real is deeply invalidating. It is tempting to keep seeking someone who will give a clear answer rather than accepting diagnostic ambiguity.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Self-educated patients who understand the imaging limitations of adhesion detection can have more productive clinical conversations. Seeking practitioners with specific experience in post-surgical pain or pelvic adhesions — rather than expecting a gastroenterologist treating an 'IBS' label to connect the dots — leads to more targeted assessment and care.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Central sensitisation can make adhesion pain persist and worsen even after the adhesions themselves are removed or become less mechanically significant. The nervous system becomes the problem.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The idea that pain can persist independently of the original structural cause feels like being told it is 'in your head.' Many people resist this because they fear it will be used to dismiss them.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Addressing central sensitisation through pain neuroscience education, psychological support, and appropriate medications does not mean abandoning the structural diagnosis. It means treating all the dimensions of a complex pain condition. People who engage with this approach typically report greater functional capacity and better quality of life, not because their adhesions are gone, but because the pain system is less amplified.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Adhesions are an almost inevitable consequence of abdominal surgery, and the medical and surgical community has historically done little to prevent them or treat them non-surgically. The problem has been systematically under-researched.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: There is a natural tendency to trust that a procedure with serious known complications would come with adequate follow-up care. Finding out that adhesion prevention and management is poorly developed in mainstream medicine can feel like a betrayal.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Seeking specialist centres with dedicated adhesion expertise — which do exist, particularly in the Netherlands, the UK, and some US centres — gives access to diagnostic tools (cine MRI) and surgical techniques with anti-adhesion barriers that are not part of routine surgical follow-up.

key_levers.lever: Specialised visceral and scar tissue physiotherapy
key_levers.why_it_matters: Even when adhesions cannot be removed, their mechanical effects on surrounding tissues can often be significantly reduced through expert manual therapy. Reducing tissue tension, improving organ mobility, and desensitising the scar region can substantially reduce pain and functional limitation over time.
key_levers.how_to_start: Search for a physiotherapist trained in visceral manipulation (Barral Institute training is a recognisable standard) or a pelvic physiotherapist with specific post-surgical and adhesion experience. Initial assessment typically involves evaluation of scar mobility and organ movement.
key_levers.timeline: Many people notice meaningful improvement after 4–8 sessions, though complex cases may require ongoing treatment over several months.

key_levers.lever: Obtaining an accurate structural assessment before any further surgery
key_levers.why_it_matters: Proceeding with repeat surgery for pain without exhausting non-surgical options significantly risks worsening the adhesion burden. A cine MRI or review at a specialist adhesion centre provides far more information than standard imaging and informs whether surgery is genuinely the best option.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask the referring clinician specifically about cine MRI for adhesion assessment, or seek a referral to a gynaecologist or colorectal surgeon with a stated interest in adhesive disease. In the UK, the Centre for Endometriosis and Minimally Invasive Surgery and the Ghent Interdisciplinary Group for Adhesion Research (GIGAR) are recognised reference points.
key_levers.timeline: Getting the right assessment may take weeks to months depending on access. The diagnostic clarity often significantly changes management.

key_levers.lever: Identifying and treating secondary SIBO or dysmotility
key_levers.why_it_matters: Adhesion-related bowel tethering can establish the conditions for persistent SIBO. Treating SIBO where present can markedly improve bloating, pain, and bowel symptoms — improving quality of life even without structural change to the adhesions.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a hydrogen and methane breath test through a gastroenterologist or functional medicine practitioner. If positive, discuss antibiotic treatment (typically rifaximin) and the need for prokinetic maintenance therapy.
key_levers.timeline: SIBO treatment typically takes 2–4 weeks; symptom improvement may be apparent within weeks but SIBO recurrence is common if the underlying dysmotility is not also managed.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The longer adhesion pain goes untreated or under-treated, the more the central nervous system adapts to amplify pain signals. Early intervention with pain neuroscience education, psychological support, and appropriate medications can slow or prevent this amplification.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a referral to a clinical psychologist specialising in chronic pain or a pain management programme. Books on pain neuroscience education (such as Explain Pain by Butler and Moseley) provide a foundational starting point for self-education.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful reduction in pain amplification typically takes months of consistent engagement with a structured programme. Some improvement in distress and function can come earlier.

key_levers.lever: Dietary management to reduce bowel symptom burden
key_levers.why_it_matters: When adhesions impair bowel motility, appropriate dietary adjustments can reduce the gas load, stool volume, and transit demands that exacerbate symptoms. This does not treat adhesions but can substantially improve day-to-day function.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult a FODMAP-trained dietitian. Begin by tracking which foods consistently worsen symptoms. Smaller, more frequent meals with lower fermentable carbohydrate content are often better tolerated.
key_levers.timeline: Dietary adjustments can show symptom benefit within 2–6 weeks if implemented consistently.

common_mistakes: Agreeing to repeat adhesiolysis surgery without a thorough non-surgical trial and specialist second opinion — each operation can worsen the adhesion burden
Treating what appears to be IBS or functional bowel disorder without reviewing post-surgical history — missing adhesions as the true driver
Stopping physiotherapy after only a few sessions without giving the treatment adequate time to show effect — scar tissue mobilisation is a slow process
Treating SIBO but not addressing the adhesion-related dysmotility that is driving recurrence — SIBO will keep returning
Accepting that nothing can be done because imaging is normal — normal CT does not exclude significant adhesive disease, and specialist assessment options exist
Framing all symptoms as psychological once organic causes are not found on standard tests — this delays appropriate management and damages the therapeutic relationship
Using high-dose opioids for long-term adhesion pain management — risk of narcotic bowel syndrome and escalating rather than resolving pain

specialists.type: Colorectal or general surgeon with adhesion expertise
specialists.role: Assessment and surgical management of bowel-related adhesions, including adhesiolysis with anti-adhesion barriers and evaluation of obstruction risk.
specialists.notes: Not all colorectal surgeons are experienced with adhesion-specific surgery. Look for surgeons who specifically mention adhesive disease or minimally invasive bowel surgery in their practice profile.

specialists.role: Assessment and surgical management of pelvic adhesions, particularly in women with a history of endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, or gynaecological surgery.
specialists.notes: Gynaecologists trained at recognised endometriosis centres typically have the most experience with complex pelvic adhesions. BSGE-accredited centres (UK), or surgeons with AAGL Minimally Invasive Gynaecology fellowship training (US), are useful starting points.

specialists.role: Non-surgical management of adhesion mechanical effects through manual therapy, scar mobilisation, and visceral manipulation.
specialists.notes: This is often the most overlooked and most accessible intervention. Practitioners should have specific training in visceral manipulation (e.g. Barral Institute) and ideally post-surgical pelvic or abdominal pain experience.

specialists.role: Assessment and treatment of pelvic floor dysfunction secondary to pelvic adhesions. Addresses pain with intercourse, pelvic heaviness, bladder symptoms, and pelvic muscular guarding.
specialists.notes: Should specifically list pelvic floor and post-surgical experience. Not the same as general musculoskeletal physiotherapy.

specialists.role: Investigation of bowel obstruction, motility disorders, and secondary conditions like SIBO. Ordering and interpreting small bowel imaging.
specialists.notes: Many gastroenterologists will manage symptoms without actively connecting them to adhesion history. It helps to explicitly raise the surgical history and ask about SIBO testing and small bowel transit studies.

specialists.type: Pain management specialist / chronic pain clinic
specialists.role: Management of central sensitisation, neuropathic pain components, and appropriate long-term analgesic strategy. Multidisciplinary programmes are particularly valuable.
specialists.notes: Look for pain clinics with experience in chronic pelvic pain or post-surgical pain specifically, as these have more relevant expertise than general pain programmes.

specialists.role: Investigation of secondary contributors such as SIBO, dysbiosis, nutritional deficiencies, and inflammation that may be amplifying adhesion-related symptoms.
specialists.notes: Mainstream clinicians often do not investigate these secondary drivers. Functional medicine can fill this gap, though quality varies significantly between practitioners. Look for practitioners with gastroenterology or pelvic pain focus.

specialists.role: Dietary management to reduce bowel symptom burden when adhesions are impairing motility and transit.
specialists.notes: Should have specific FODMAP training and ideally experience with post-surgical gut patients. General dietary advice is usually insufficient for this complexity.

patient_communities.name: Adhesions.org (International Adhesions Society)
patient_communities.notes: One of the longest-standing patient resources specifically focused on surgical adhesions. Contains patient forums, medical information, and a directory of clinicians with adhesion expertise.

patient_communities.name: r/adhesions
patient_communities.notes: A small but active community of people sharing experiences with surgical adhesions, including diagnostic journeys, surgical decisions, and physiotherapy. Useful for peer support and practical questions.

patient_communities.name: Endometriosis UK
patient_communities.notes: Relevant for those with pelvic adhesions related to endometriosis. Strong support network, helpline, and clinician directory including BSGE-accredited centres.

patient_communities.name: Pelvic Pain Support Network (PPSN)
patient_communities.notes: UK-based but internationally relevant. Covers pelvic adhesions among the causes of chronic pelvic pain. Includes patient stories, clinician resources, and a helpline.

patient_communities.name: Adhesion Related Disorder (ARD) Facebook Group
patient_communities.notes: Search 'Adhesion Related Disorder' on Facebook. Active patient group sharing experiences, surgeon recommendations, and research. Particularly useful for finding patients who have navigated specialist adhesion surgery.

learning_resources.title: International Adhesions Society — Patient Education
learning_resources.description: The most comprehensive patient-facing resource specifically dedicated to surgical adhesions. Covers mechanisms, symptoms, diagnostic approaches, surgical options, and clinician directory. An essential starting point for newly diagnosed or suspected adhesion patients.

learning_resources.title: Adhesions: A Hidden Cause of Chronic Pain
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various; search PubMed for 'peritoneal adhesions chronic pain'
learning_resources.description: A body of peer-reviewed literature examining the relationship between post-surgical adhesions and chronic pain, bowel obstruction, and infertility. Useful for patients who want to understand the science behind their condition and present evidence to clinicians.

learning_resources.title: Explain Pain
learning_resources.description: Not specific to adhesions, but essential reading for anyone with chronic pain from structural causes that is not resolving. Explains in accessible terms how the nervous system amplifies pain and what can be done about it. Relevant to those developing central sensitisation secondary to adhesion pain.

learning_resources.title: Cine-MRI for Detection of Peritoneal Adhesions
learning_resources.author_or_source: Lieneman A et al. / various research groups
learning_resources.description: Research papers describing the use of dynamic MRI to detect adhesions non-invasively. Useful for patients wanting to understand this emerging diagnostic technique and advocate for its use with their clinician.

learning_resources.title: Pelvic Pain: The Ultimate Cock-Up (Channel 4 / UK Documentaries on Endometriosis and Pelvic Pain)
learning_resources.description: UK documentary coverage of the chronic pelvic pain and endometriosis diagnostic delay crisis. While not adhesion-specific, it contextualises the systemic failures in pelvic pain diagnosis that affect adhesion patients disproportionately.

learning_resources.title: Visceral Manipulation
learning_resources.description: The foundational text for visceral manipulation physiotherapy by its developer. Helps patients understand the principles behind this form of manual therapy and what a trained visceral physiotherapist assesses and treats. Relevant for those considering this approach for adhesion management.

learning_resources.title: SIBO — Small Intestine Bacterial Overgrowth (Dr. Allison Siebecker)
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive patient and clinician resource on SIBO, which is a common secondary complication of adhesion-related bowel dysmotility. Covers testing, treatment protocols, and the structural causes of SIBO recurrence including post-surgical adhesions.

sections.relevance_signal: **Symptoms began or worsened after abdominal surgery** — appendectomy, C-section, hysterectomy, bowel surgery. Constipation with cramping and a feeling of obstruction.
